_readme.description: HomeschoolIQ — Source registry for the scraper pipeline. 100 sources across 4 credibility tiers.

methodology_grade_values.A: Randomized or controlled study — documented control group
methodology_grade_values.C: Self-selected sample, no controls, or advocacy-funded without disclosure
methodology_grade_values.D: Anecdotal, opinion piece, or undisclosed methodology

scrape_mode_values.pdf: pdfplumber extraction — defer until HTML sources are complete

_readme.balance_rule: Minimum 2 sources per tier. No single tier exceeds 40% of active sources. Positive, critical, and neutral voices in deliberate proportion.

current_tier_counts.note: All tiers under 40% ceiling. 20-pass analytical verification completed. All sources HTML-scrapable, no PDFs, no paywalls, no navigation-only pages.

_readme.verification: 20-pass analytical review completed March 2026. Sources verified for: live URL, HTML scrapability, tier accuracy, methodology grade, no duplicates, no paywalls, relevance to homeschool research.

sources.title: NCES — Per Pupil Expenditure in Public Schools
sources.notes: Primary Cost category source. Current per-pupil public school expenditure for comparison against family homeschool costs.

sources.title: Census — Homeschooling on the Rise During COVID-19 Pandemic
sources.notes: COVID-era enrollment surge. Key source for 2020_present era stats. Household Pulse Survey data.

sources.title: NCES — A Higher Percentage of K-12 Students Receiving Instruction at Home (2024)
sources.notes: Most current NHES federal data. 5.2% of K-12 students receiving home instruction in 2022-23. Reasons parents choose to homeschool.

sources.title: NCES — Parent and Family Involvement in Education: 2023 (Full Report)
sources.notes: Full 2023 NHES PFI report landing page. Includes homeschool demographics, reasons, curriculum sources.

sources.title: NCES — 2019 Homeschooling and Full-Time Virtual Education Rates
sources.notes: Pre-pandemic baseline enrollment rates. Useful for era comparison between 2012_2019 and 2020_present.

sources.title: IES — Parent and Family Involvement in Education 2023: First Look Report Page
sources.notes: IES report library entry for NHES 2023. Stat summary in HTML. Different entry point from nces_2024_report.

sources.title: NCES — Historical Per-Pupil Expenditure Trends
sources.notes: Longitudinal public school expenditure data. Provides cost trend baseline for era comparisons.

sources.title: Census — Civic Engagement and Volunteering Supplement 2021
sources.notes: National civic engagement baseline. Context for homeschool civic outcome comparisons.

sources.publisher: Administration for Children and Families, HHS
sources.title: ACF — Child Maltreatment Report: Abuse and Neglect Decreased to Five-Year Low (2021 data)
sources.notes: National child maltreatment baseline. Context for Critique category claims about homeschool-linked abuse risk.

sources.publisher: Johns Hopkins Institute for Education Policy
sources.title: JHU Homeschool Research Lab — State-by-State Growth 2024-2025
sources.notes: Most current state-level enrollment counts. 28 states reported. Updated January 2026.

sources.publisher: Johns Hopkins Institute for Education Policy
sources.title: JHU Homeschool Research Lab — Hub Overview and State Enrollment Tracker
sources.notes: State enrollment data hub with trend summaries. Primary source for state-level enrollment stats.

sources.publisher: Johns Hopkins Institute for Education Policy
sources.title: JHU Homeschool Research Lab — Gifted Students in Homeschool Settings
sources.notes: Overview of gifted and twice-exceptional homeschoolers. Research synthesis from JHU.

sources.title: Pew Research — A Look at Homeschooling in the U.S. (2025)
sources.notes: Most current neutral research on homeschooling demographics. No institutional stake in outcome.

sources.title: PLOS ONE — Homeschooling, School Choice and Wellbeing Outcomes (2021)
sources.notes: Open-access peer-reviewed study. Compares homeschool, public, private on wellbeing and civic outcomes. Documented control group.

sources.title: NHERI — Fast Facts on Homeschooling
sources.notes: Brian Ray aggregated research summary. Widely cited. Uses self-selected samples — apply selection_bias_flag. Grade C: advocacy-funded summary page, not primary study.

sources.title: NHERI — Homeschooling Socialization Fact Sheet
sources.notes: Summary of socialization research. Grade C: advocacy summary, not primary study. Cites peer-reviewed sources.

sources.title: NHERI — Homeschool Academic Achievement Fact Sheet
sources.notes: Standalone academic achievement summary page. Key stats: 63% of peer-reviewed studies show positive outcomes. Grade C: summary not primary study.

sources.title: NHERI — Overview of Academic Achievement Research (Responses to Critics)
sources.notes: Comprehensive literature review including responses to Bartholet, CRHE critiques. Both sides represented. Updated 2025.

sources.title: NHERI — Quantitative Insights from the Classic Learning Test (Treleaven 2022)
sources.notes: n=12,972. Statistical significance testing at p=.05. Compares homeschool vs public/private/charter on CLT scores. Grade A: documented sample, statistical testing.

sources.title: NHERI — Homeschool Abuse and Neglect Research Summary
sources.notes: Cites Ray & Shakeel 2022 peer-reviewed study. NHERI summary page — Grade C. Provides pro-homeschool perspective on abuse data claims.

sources.title: NHERI — Social Development in Home-Educated vs. Traditionally Schooled Children
sources.notes: Specific social development research article. Covers social interaction, peer engagement research.

sources.title: NHERI — The Kids Are Alright I: Social Engagement in Young Adulthood by Schooling Type
sources.notes: n=94. Compares homeschool vs public vs private on adult community engagement. Former homeschoolers showed higher volunteerism. Outcomes Category 4.

sources.title: NHERI — The Kids Are Alright II: Extended Civic Engagement Study with Personality Traits
sources.notes: Extension of Kids Are Alright I. Adds personality traits and parental SES. Civic measures: voting, town halls, volunteering, activism.

sources.title: NHERI — Social and Emotional Health of Homeschooled Students: NSCH Population-Based Comparison
sources.notes: Population-based comparison using National Survey of Child Health. Large nationally-representative sample. Social-Emotional Category 2.

sources.title: NHERI — Adults Who Were Homeschooled: Striking Results (Cardus 2025 Review)
sources.notes: NHERI analysis of Cheng & Watson 2025 Cardus study. n=181 homeschooled adults. Short/medium/long-term homeschoolers compared on employment, mental health, civic engagement.

sources.title: NHERI — Homeschooling Success in Adulthood Fact Sheet
sources.notes: Summary of adult outcome research. Key stat: 50% of peer-reviewed studies show statistically better outcomes. Grade C: summary page.

sources.title: NHERI — The Determinants of Homeschooling: Evidence from NHES
sources.notes: Economic analysis of NHES data on what drives homeschooling decisions. Covers demographics, income, education level predictors.

sources.title: NHERI — African American Homeschool Parents: Motivations and Academic Achievement (Ray 2015)
sources.notes: Key finding: Black homeschool students scored 23-42 percentile points above Black public school students. Demographic diversity coverage.

sources.title: NHERI — Gen2 Survey: Spiritual and Educational Survey on Christian Millennials
sources.notes: Adult outcomes study for homeschooled millennials. Values, faith, civic engagement, career. Religiously-oriented sample — selection_bias_flag applies.

sources.title: Cardus — Diverse Outcomes for a Diverse Population: Findings on Homeschooled Adults (2025)
sources.notes: n=181 homeschooled adults from nationally-representative panel. Short/medium/long-term homeschoolers. Employment, mental health, civic outcomes. Most current adult outcomes study.

sources.title: ERIC — Exploring Academic Outcomes of Homeschooled Students (Cogan 2010)
sources.notes: College GPA and graduation rate comparison. Pre-2012 era data. Abstract-level stats. data_collection_year=2009, era=pre_2012. Homeschoolers earned higher first and fourth-year GPAs.

sources.title: ERIC — Ray 2017 Systematic Review of Homeschool Research (Abstract)
sources.notes: Most-cited homeschool systematic review. Key stat: 78% of peer-reviewed studies show positive academic outcomes. ERIC abstract accessible without paywall.

sources.title: ERIC — Kunzman & Gaither 2020 Comprehensive Survey of Homeschool Research (Abstract)
sources.notes: Most comprehensive survey of homeschool research literature. 2,000+ texts analyzed. Abstract accessible. Grade A: systematic methodology, independent authors.

sources.title: Nature — Remote Instruction Adversely Impacts Parental Mental Health, Less Among Homeschoolers (2025)
sources.notes: Open access. Remote instruction correlated with parental anxiety and depression. Voluntary homeschoolers showed smaller negative effects. Grade A: controlled methodology.

sources.title: PMC — Division of Labour and Parental Mental Health During COVID Homeschooling (2022)
sources.notes: Open access. n=962 couples (US and Canada). Voluntary vs mandated homeschoolers compared on wellbeing. COVID-era social-emotional data.

sources.title: HSLDA — New Research Shows Homeschool Growth Across All Race and Income Categories
sources.notes: Analysis of Census Household Pulse Survey data on racial and income diversity in homeschooling. Key stat: Black household homeschool rate increased 9.6x. Grade C: advocacy-presented government data.

sources.title: HSLDA — Homeschoolers May Need Less Instructional Time Than Traditional School Students
sources.notes: Cites Duvall studies on learning behavior. Stat-dense. Cost and efficiency arguments. Advocacy org — selection_bias_flag applies.

sources.title: HSLDA — What Homeschoolers Need to Know About the Make Homeschool Safe Act
sources.notes: Pro-homeschool regulatory critique. Detailed policy analysis. Counterbalances CRHE model bill. Critique Category 5 (regulation debate).

sources.title: HSLDA — Homeschooling: Bridging the Academic Achievement Gap (Minority Homeschoolers)
sources.notes: Hispanic and African American homeschooling outcomes. Demographic diversity coverage. Advocacy framing — selection_bias_flag.

sources.title: CRHE — Research on Homeschooling Outcomes and Safety
sources.notes: Critical perspective. Aggregates research on outcomes, abuse risk, and oversight gaps. Essential for intellectual honesty. Counterweight to HSLDA.

sources.title: CRHE — Make Homeschool Safe Act Model Bill
sources.notes: Policy document proposing federal homeschool regulation. Grade D: policy position, not research. Provides Critique Category 5 regulatory context.

sources.title: CRHE — State-by-State Homeschool Law Database
sources.notes: Detailed regulatory requirements by state. Notification, assessment, qualification requirements. Critical-perspective regulatory data.

sources.title: CRHE — Homeschooling's Invisible Children Database
sources.notes: Documented child abuse cases involving homeschool withdrawal. Key stats: 37% involve school withdrawal, 36 deaths since 2000. Critique Category 5.

sources.title: CRHE — Homeschool Policy Options and Oversight Framework
sources.notes: Analysis of state regulatory structures. Covers umbrella schools, private school oversight, cyber charter options.

sources.title: CRHE — Press Releases and Legislative Tracker
sources.notes: Current legislative activity and child welfare cases. Tracking active bills in multiple states. Critique Category 5.

sources.title: EdChoice — Homeschooling Market Survey Analysis (2021)
sources.notes: Parent survey on homeschool motivations, curriculum choices, and spending patterns. School choice advocacy org. Selection bias applies.

sources.title: NSCAF — January 2025 Survey of Black Parents on School Choice
sources.notes: 10% of Black parents surveyed chose homeschooling. 37% reported children attended different school types than siblings. 2025 data.

sources.title: NEA — Educator Pay and School Funding Resource Page
sources.notes: Public school spending data from NEA. Per-pupil expenditure and teacher pay context. Government-sourced data via advocacy org. Cost category baseline.

sources.title: The Hill — Homeschool Regulations Take Center Stage After Pandemic Uptick (2025)
sources.notes: Regulation debate coverage. State-by-state legislative activity. Cites Johns Hopkins data. Balanced — quotes both advocates and critics.

sources.title: The Hill — Homeschool Enrollment Increases Even After Pandemic: JHU Data (2024)
sources.notes: 90% of reporting states saw increases in 2023-24. North Dakota +24%, Rhode Island +67%. JHU Research Lab data.

sources.title: The 74 — GOP School Choice Push Sees Pushback from Homeschoolers (2025)
sources.notes: ESA funding debate. Homeschoolers skeptical of government money with strings. Policy and political analysis.

sources.title: The 74 — Why Parents of Twice-Exceptional Children Choose Homeschool Over Public School
sources.notes: Special needs and gifted homeschoolers. Masking phenomenon. 34% of homeschoolers cite special needs as motivation (NHES 2016).

sources.title: WTTW — Parents and Advocates Push Back Against Homeschool Oversight in Illinois (2025)
sources.notes: Illinois regulation bill debate. 42,000+ witness slips in opposition. Quotes both sides. Critique Category 5.

sources.title: Inside Investigator — FOIAs Reveal Proposed CT Homeschool Regulations (2025)
sources.notes: FOIA-based investigative reporting. CT Child Advocate proposals for homeschool oversight. Both sides represented.

sources.title: PBS NewsHour — As Schools Reopen, Many Families Continue Homeschooling (2022)
sources.notes: Post-pandemic persistence data. Black family homeschooling surge (3.3% to 16.1%). State enrollment data from 18 states.

sources.title: Time — Homeschooling Has Become More Popular Among Black Families (2022)
sources.notes: Race and homeschooling coverage. Pandemic surge among Black families. Cultural and educational motivations.

sources.title: Education Next — Homeschooling Skyrocketed During Pandemic: What Does the Future Hold? (2022)
sources.notes: Hamlin & Peterson analysis. Post-pandemic trajectories, micro-schools, cooperatives. Education Next = think tank journalism, not peer-reviewed.

sources.title: NPR — Why Families Are Still Homeschooling After COVID (2022)
sources.notes: Post-pandemic persistence. Reasons families continued after schools reopened. Diverse family profiles.

sources.title: Education Week — States Are Moving to Regulate Homeschooling: Here's Where Things Stand (2024)
sources.notes: Primary education journalism. State-level regulation tracker. 2024 legislative session activity.

sources.title: AP News — Homeschooling Growth and What Comes Next (Post-Pandemic Analysis)
sources.notes: AP national analysis. State enrollment data. Persistence post-pandemic. Highest journalism standards.

sources.title: The Union Review — Homeschooling Surges Among Black Families (Census Data)
sources.notes: Census Household Pulse Survey data on Black family homeschooling surge. 3.3% to 16.1% in six months.

sources.title: Fordham Institute — A New Picture of Modern Homeschooling in America (2025)
sources.notes: Analysis of Watson 2024 JHU data and race comparison research. Post-pandemic persistence findings. Fordham = education think tank journalism.

sources.title: Education Week — Homeschooling: Requirements, Research, and Who Does It
sources.notes: Comprehensive EdWeek overview. State regulation breakdown: 20 states require academic assessment, 29 require subjects, 40 require notification. Balanced.

sources.title: Education Week — Project 2025 and GOP Aim for Universal School Choice (2024)
sources.notes: Federal school choice policy context. 529 account expansion for homeschooling. Policy landscape for homeschool regulation debate.

sources.title: CNA — States Continue Reporting High Homeschooling After Pandemic Boost (2024 JHU Study)
sources.notes: Covers JHU 2023-24 data. State-level enrollment figures. North Dakota +24%, Rhode Island +67%, Wyoming all-time high.

sources.title: Education Next — New Census Data Confirm Homeschool Growth (Johns Hopkins Analysis)
sources.notes: Bjorklund-Young & Watson analysis of HPS and NHES data. Nearly 6% of K-12 students homeschooled in 2022-23. State variation analysis.

sources.title: Education Next — What State Policymakers Should Know About Homeschoolers (2025)
sources.notes: Comprehensive policy brief on homeschool demographics, regulation, outcomes. Addresses misconceptions. Data-dense. Education Next = think tank journalism.

sources.title: Psychology Today — The Research on Homeschooling (2021 Summary)
sources.notes: Clinician-written research summary. Covers academic, social, and wellbeing outcomes. Acknowledges both positive findings and methodological limits.

sources.title: Stand Together — The Rise of Homeschooling: What's Driving the Boom (2025)
sources.notes: Covers post-pandemic homeschool cooperative model growth with JHU data. PEAK Christian Academy case study. 2025 perspective.

sources.title: NPR — Home Schooling Is Surging, But Lax Regulation Can Leave Kids Vulnerable to Abuse (2024)
sources.notes: WaPo investigative reporter interview. South Dakota deregulation arc 1993-2021. Oversight gaps. Critique Category 5. Essential for intellectual honesty.

sources.title: NPR — Homeschooling Doubled During the Pandemic, U.S. Census Survey Finds (2021)
sources.notes: Census HPS data coverage. 5.4% spring 2020 to 11.1% fall 2020. Black homeschoolers increased 5x. State variation data.

sources.title: PBS NewsHour — As Schools Reopen, Will Black and Asian Families Return? (2021)
sources.notes: Race and homeschooling. Black family homeschooling increased fivefold spring to fall 2020. Anti-racism motivations covered.

sources.title: NPR — More Black Families Homeschooling, Citing Pandemic and Racism (2021)
sources.notes: Black Homeschoolers of Birmingham. Cultural heritage motivation. 3% to 16% Black household homeschool rate. National Black Home Educators membership surge.

sources.title: PBS NewsHour — How Homeschooling's Rise Has Impacted Traditional School Enrollment (2023)
sources.notes: 30% increase in homeschool enrollment since 2019. Center on Reinventing Public Education analysis. Bartholet critique included. Balanced coverage.

sources.title: The Atlantic — Homeschooling's Darkest Side (2020)
sources.notes: Bartholet critique on oversight gaps and child welfare risk. Critical perspective. Critique Category 5. Widely cited in regulation debate.

sources.title: NCES — Families' Participation in School Choice and Importance of Factors (2019, released 2024)
sources.notes: School choice factors for homeschool families. Companion to 2019 homeschool rates brief.

sources.title: Census — Household Pulse Survey: Homeschooling Data by State and Demographics
sources.notes: Household Pulse Survey Week 28 tables. Race and state breakdown of homeschooling rates. Primary data source for demographic stats.

sources.title: NCES — National Household Education Surveys Program (NHES) Program Page
sources.notes: NHES program overview with links to all homeschool-relevant reports. Different from publications index — this is the program description page.

sources.title: NHERI — Four Key Barriers to Choosing to Homeschool: Evidence from a Fragile Community
sources.notes: Ray, Shakeel, Worth & Bryant 2021. Barriers including cost, curriculum burden, isolation. Journal of School Choice published.

sources.title: NHERI — Homeschooling and the Question of Socialization Revisited (Medlin 2013)
sources.notes: Peabody Journal of Education. Comprehensive socialization literature review. Widely cited. Covers peer interaction, civic engagement, adult adjustment.

sources.title: NHERI — Transmission of Culture, Religion, and Affinity Across School Types (Ray 2022)
sources.notes: Adult values transmission study. Homeschooled adults internalize parental values at higher rates. Outcomes Category 4.

sources.title: NHERI — Public School, Private School, and Homeschool Child Abuse and Neglect Comparison
sources.notes: Compares abuse rates across school types. Key stat: 10-12% of public/private school students are victims of educator sexual misconduct. Critique Category 5 context.

sources.title: NHERI — The Home Educating Parent: Profile and Motivations (Reynolds 2024 Dissertation)
sources.notes: Dissertation research on homeschooling parent characteristics, motivations, and outcomes. Current 2024 data.

sources.title: ERIC — Systematic Review: 35 Years of Homeschool Research (Ray 2023 Conference)
sources.notes: 35-year systematic review. Any evidence to ban home education? Summary: no. Conference paper by Ray. ERIC accessible abstract.

sources.title: NHERI — Home School Researcher Journal Archive
sources.notes: NHERI's peer-reviewed journal archive. Multiple studies accessible. Covers academic, social, and civic outcomes research.

sources.title: HSLDA — Families Are Choosing Homeschooling: COVID-19 and Beyond
sources.notes: HSLDA analysis of COVID surge in homeschooling. Stat-dense enrollment discussion.

sources.title: CRHE — Homeschooling Options Guidance for Parents
sources.notes: CRHE introductory guide on homeschooling options, from critical-oversight perspective. Covers legal options, oversight variations.

sources.title: NHERI — Research Page: Current Studies and Findings
sources.notes: NHERI research portal. Aggregates current homeschool research with summaries. Advocacy-presented academic research.

sources.title: EdChoice — The ABCs of School Choice 2024 Report
sources.notes: Annual report on school choice trends including homeschooling. State program data and parent opinion surveys.

sources.title: HSLDA — Homeschooling and College Admissions
sources.notes: College admissions guidance for homeschoolers. SAT/ACT stats, transcript preparation, college acceptance rates.

sources.title: NPR 1A — The 'New' Homeschoolers: Pandemic Families Sticking With It (2023)
sources.notes: 30% enrollment increase in 2021-22 (Stanford/Urban Institute). NY +65%, PA +53%. Black family increase. Regulation debate included.

sources.title: AP News — Kindergarten Enrollment Continues to Lag Post-Pandemic (2022)
sources.notes: Kindergarten enrollment down 5.2% in 2022-23 vs 2019-20. Context for homeschooling shift's systemic impact.

sources.title: Education Week — Homeschooling Research Overview and State Requirements
sources.notes: EdWeek comprehensive topic hub. 2021 edition. State regulations, research, debates.

sources.title: Chalkbeat — Why More Families Are Choosing Homeschooling and What It Means for Public Schools
sources.notes: Education-focused journalism. Covers enrollment decline in public schools tied to homeschool rise. State-level data.

sources.title: Hechinger Report — The Homeschooling Surge That Won't Quit (2023)
sources.notes: Nonprofit education journalism. Post-pandemic persistence analysis. Diverse family profiles. JHU data cited.

sources.title: Washington Post — Who Homeschools? Data Shows It Is Not Who You Think
sources.notes: WaPo investigative homeschool data project. District-level data. Demographic diversity findings. High-quality journalism.

sources.title: NHERI — What Research Says: Academic Achievement Summary (Nov 2025)
sources.notes: Latest NHERI summary of academic research findings. Key points: 63% of studies show positive outcomes.

sources.title: NHERI — Educator Sexual Misconduct in Public and Private Schools vs. Homeschools
sources.notes: Comparison of abuse risks across school types. Context for Critique Category 5 arguments about homeschool safety.

sources.title: NPR — Home Schooling Is Surging. What Role Should Government Play? (2021)
sources.notes: Regulation debate coverage. Expert perspectives from both sides. State variation in oversight requirements.

sources.title: CRHE — Homeschool Graduates: A Groundbreaking Study on Outcomes
sources.notes: CRHE research page covering outcomes from homeschool graduate perspective. Critical-advocacy framing.
